description: Brachytherapy domain knowledge base for RAG retrieval

chunks.text: CTV (Clinical Target Volume) includes the visible tumor and suspected subclinical disease spread. It is defined by the radiation oncologist based on imaging and clinical examination.
OAR (Organ at Risk) refers to normal tissues that may be affected by radiation dose. Common OARs in brachytherapy include rectum, bladder, urethra, bowel, and spinal cord.
V100 represents the percentage of the target volume receiving at least 100% of the prescribed dose. A V100 >= 90% is generally considered acceptable for prostate brachytherapy.
D90 is the minimum dose covering 90% of the target volume. For I-125 prostate implants, D90 should be >= 140 Gy (post-implant).
V150 represents the percentage of the target volume receiving at least 150% of the prescribed dose. V150 should generally be < 60% to avoid excessive hot spots.
V200 represents the percentage of the target volume receiving at least 200% of the prescribed dose. V200 should generally be < 35% to minimize tissue necrosis risk.
D2cc is the minimum dose covering the hottest 2 cubic centimeters of an organ at risk. It is a standard metric for OAR dose constraints in brachytherapy.
Rectum dose constraints: D2cc < 75 Gy (EQD2) for prostate brachytherapy. Exceeding this increases risk of rectal toxicity and fistula.
Bladder dose constraints: D2cc < 90 Gy (EQD2) for prostate brachytherapy. Urethra D10 < 150% of prescribed dose.
Seed placement patterns: peripheral loading (seeds placed near the periphery of the target) provides more homogeneous dose distribution compared to uniform loading.
Trajectory planning optimizes needle paths to minimize OAR exposure while ensuring adequate target coverage. Parallel catheters are typically used for dose uniformity.
Pd-103 and I-125 are the most common isotopes for permanent prostate brachytherapy. Pd-103 has a shorter half-life (17 days) vs I-125 (60 days).
Intra-operative replanning is triggered when seed deviation exceeds the threshold (typically 2-3 mm). The system detects implanted seeds and adjusts the remaining plan.
DVH (Dose Volume Histogram) is a cumulative histogram showing the volume of a structure receiving at least a given dose. It is the primary tool for plan evaluation.

BrachyBot's only supported dose calculation engine is the CNN surrogate (myDoseNet) — a deep-learning model that predicts the 3D dose distribution around a seed given the local CT context. The earlier analytical Gaussian fallback has been removed because its 1/r^2 approximation was not clinically reliable; always rely on the CNN engine for dose evaluation and replanning.
